patterns.note: non-mutating "derive a new value" accessor — the receiver is left untouched

methods.rationale: Returns a brand-new NSString that the caller owns; the receiver and the argument are only read, so the argument is "borrowed" — it's neither copied nor retained beyond the call — and because it's pure string arithmetic it's safe on any thread.

patterns.note: in-place insert/replace on a mutable collection

methods.rationale: Classic dictionary "put": the value is retained for storage while the key is copied — Foundation copies keys so a later mutation of the caller's key object can't silently re-bucket the entry; the receiver is mutated in place and nothing is returned.

patterns.note: blocking disk write; "atomically" routes through a temp file -> rename

methods.rationale: Returns a BOOL success flag and reports detail through the trailing NSError** out-parameter — the convention is "check the BOOL first, only then read *error"; the URL argument is merely borrowed for the duration of the synchronous write.

patterns.note: edits the receiver's backing store in place — no copy, no new object

methods.rationale: This mutates the receiver in place rather than returning a new string, so it carries the usual mutable-object caveat: it's only safe when the caller owns the thread driving the mutation — concurrent readers/writers must serialize externally; the replacement string is just read.

patterns.note: returns a sorted copy; original array is unchanged

methods.rationale: The comparator runs synchronously — it's called repeatedly during the sort and never retained past return, so it does not "escape"; the method hands back a freshly-allocated sorted array that the caller owns.

patterns.note: block-based observer registration; pairs with removeObserver: -> caller must unregister

methods.rationale: The block is "stored": the center holds onto it and fires it later — possibly many times — on the supplied queue, so it long-outlives the call; the return value is an opaque observer token the caller owns and is responsible for handing to removeObserver: (leak otherwise).

patterns.note: Swift structured-concurrency request -> awaits the full response in memory

methods.rationale: A Swift `async` call that suspends and resumes with the tuple (Data, URLResponse) the caller owns; "can throw" maps to Swift's typed/untyped throws — modeled here as the exception error pattern rather than an NSError** out-param — and being async it imposes no thread affinity of its own.

patterns.note: Combine operator -> returns a derived publisher that fuses the latest values

methods.rationale: The trailing transform closure is "stored" for the lifetime of the returned publisher — every time any upstream emits, the operator re-invokes it with the latest tuple — so it escapes the call entirely; the result is a new publisher value owned by the caller's subscription graph.

patterns.note: AppKit window activation -> touches the window server; main-thread-bound

methods.rationale: It mutates on-screen UI state — making the window key and bringing it forward — so like nearly all AppKit it is "main-thread-only"; the `sender` argument is just an identity tag that's borrowed, not held.

patterns.note: directory enumeration; nil sentinel distinguishes "empty" from "failed"

methods.rationale: Returns an autoreleased-but-caller-owned NSArray of URLs, or nil on failure — the "nil-means-error, then read the NSError**" idiom — so it carries the nserror_out pattern; the directory URL is borrowed for the read only.

patterns.note: Key-Value Coding traversal -> walks dotted key paths via the runtime

methods.rationale: KVC's read side: an unknown key path triggers `valueForUndefinedKey:` whose default raises an NSException — hence the "exception" error pattern rather than a returned error — and the value handed back is borrowed (autoreleased, owned by the object graph, not freshly minted for the caller).

patterns.note: advances the scanner's cursor on match -> stateful, order-dependent

methods.rationale: Returns YES/NO and writes the matched text through the NSString** out-param — but that out-param is "valid only when it returns YES", the textbook "check the BOOL before touching the by-reference result" contract; it advances the receiver's internal scan location, so concurrent use of one scanner must be serialized to its owning thread.

patterns.note: convenience constructor -> returns an autoreleased instance per Cocoa naming rules

methods.rationale: A class-method factory: by the Cocoa "a method whose name doesn't begin with alloc/new/copy/create returns an autoreleased object" rule, the caller gets back an autoreleased NSColor it does not own — it must retain it to keep it past the pool drain.

patterns.note: synchronous per-match callback with a `stop` flag -> not re-entrant

methods.rationale: The block fires synchronously once per match and is never stored, so it doesn't escape — but there's a sharp re-entrancy rule: "the block must not call back into the regex" while enumeration is in flight, otherwise the matcher's cursor state is corrupted; the subject string is read-only here.

patterns.note: fire-and-forget submission -> runs once, later, on a GCD worker

methods.rationale: The work item is "@escaping": dispatch enqueues it and returns immediately, then runs it once later on one of the queue's private worker threads — so it both outlives the call and executes off the submitting thread; you can hand it off from any thread.

patterns.note: publishes pasteboard-writable objects -> they persist until the next declareTypes:/clearContents

methods.rationale: The elements of the passed array are retained by the pasteboard — it holds them to service later paste/read requests, so the caller can release its own references; returns a BOOL but no owned object.

patterns.note: view-hierarchy graft -> parent owns child; touches layout/display

methods.rationale: The parent view retains the child it adopts — that's the ownership edge of the view hierarchy — and because it mutates the on-screen layout tree it's main-thread-only like the rest of AppKit.

patterns.note: builds a suspended task -> caller must call -resume; completion fires exactly once

methods.rationale: The completion handler is "@escaping": it's stored and called exactly once, later, on a background delegate queue — never on the caller's thread — so don't touch UI from inside it without hopping to main; the request is copied defensively, and the returned task is a caller-owned object that starts suspended (you must `resume` it).

patterns.note: path-based blocking disk write -> the "atomically" temp-file dance
superseded by writeToURL:atomically:encoding:error: — prefer the URL form

methods.rationale: Same BOOL + NSError** contract as its URL sibling, but it's the deprecated path-string variant — Apple steers callers to writeToURL:atomically:encoding:error: instead; the file path is borrowed for the synchronous write only.

patterns.note: date -> localized string; the formatter caches mutable state across calls

methods.rationale: Despite "just formatting", NSDateFormatter is not thread-safe — the receiver carries mutable caches it touches per call, so a single formatter shared across threads must be externally synchronized (hence "owning-thread-only"); the returned string is freshly produced and owned by the caller, and the date is only read.
